title: SHAP
definition: A game theory-based approach that assigns each feature a Shapley value representing its contribution to the difference between the actual prediction and the average prediction. Provides both local (per-prediction) and global (overall) explanations.
why_used: The gold standard for ML explainability. Theoretically grounded in Shapley values from cooperative game theory, satisfying desirable properties: Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, and Linearity. Works with any model.
interview_point: SHAP Properties: 1) Efficiency: SHAP values sum to the prediction minus baseline. 2) Consistency: if model relies more on a feature, its SHAP value increases. 3) Dummy: features not affecting the model get SHAP=0. Positive SHAP = feature pushed prediction up. Negative SHAP = pushed it down.
limitations: Computationally expensive for large datasets or complex models
TreeExplainer is fast; KernelExplainer is very slow
real_world_example:
```python
import shap
from sklearn.ensemble import XGBClassifier

model = XGBClassifier().fit(X_train, y_train)

# Fast TreeExplainer for tree-based models
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)

# Global: feature importance summary
shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_test, feature_names=feature_names)

# Local: explain a single prediction
shap.waterfall_plot(shap.Explanation(
    values=shap_values[0],
    base_values=explainer.expected_value,
    data=X_test.iloc[0],
    feature_names=feature_names
))

# Feature interaction
shap.dependence_plot('age', shap_values, X_test)
```

title: LIME
definition: A model-agnostic technique that explains individual predictions by training a simple interpretable model (linear) locally around the data point being explained, perturbing it and observing how the model's output changes.
why_used: Provides local explanations for any black-box model — useful for understanding why a specific prediction was made, regardless of the model type.
interview_point: LIME vs SHAP: LIME is faster for complex models but less theoretically grounded and can give inconsistent explanations (different runs may give different results due to random sampling). SHAP is more reliable with theoretical guarantees. LIME requires careful selection of the neighborhood size for the local model.
real_world_example:
```python
import lime
import lime.lime_tabular

explainer = lime.lime_tabular.LimeTabularExplainer(
    training_data=X_train.values,
    feature_names=feature_names,
    class_names=['No Fraud', 'Fraud'],
    mode='classification'
)

# Explain a single prediction
instance = X_test.iloc[0].values
explanation = explainer.explain_instance(
    data_row=instance,
    predict_fn=model.predict_proba,
    num_features=10   # Show top 10 features
)
explanation.show_in_notebook()
explanation.as_list()  # [(feature, weight), ...]
```

title: Grad-CAM
definition: A visual explanation technique for CNN-based models that uses the gradients of the target class flowing into the last convolutional layer to produce a coarse localization map highlighting important image regions.
why_used: Shows WHICH parts of an image a CNN looked at when making its prediction. Essential for debugging CV models and understanding whether the model focuses on correct image regions.
interview_point: Grad-CAM computes gradients of the class score with respect to the last conv layer's feature maps. Global average pooling of these gradients gives importance weights per feature map channel. These weights create a heatmap when combined with feature maps. It does NOT require retraining or model modification.
advantages: Class-discriminative (shows why this class, not others)
limitations: Low spatial resolution (from last conv layer)
Coarse — cannot localize small objects precisely
use_cases: Detecting if model focuses on correct regions
real_world_example:
```python
from pytorch_grad_cam import GradCAM
from pytorch_grad_cam.utils.image import show_cam_on_image
from pytorch_grad_cam.utils.model_targets import ClassifierOutputTarget
import torchvision.models as models

model = models.resnet50(weights='DEFAULT')
model.eval()

# Target the last conv layer
target_layers = [model.layer4[-1]]
cam = GradCAM(model=model, target_layers=target_layers)

# Generate CAM for class 'dog' (class index 208)
targets = [ClassifierOutputTarget(208)]
grayscale_cam = cam(input_tensor=input_tensor, targets=targets)

# Overlay on original image
visualization = show_cam_on_image(rgb_img, grayscale_cam[0], use_rgb=True)
```

title: Permutation Importance
definition: Measures feature importance by randomly shuffling one feature at a time and measuring the drop in model performance. A large performance drop = the feature is important.
why_used: More reliable than tree-based feature importance — unbiased toward high-cardinality features, model-agnostic, and measured on actual validation performance.
interview_point: Permutation Importance is measured on VALIDATION data (not training) — it captures what the model actually relies on for generalization. If correlated features are both shuffled independently, their importance is underestimated. SHAP better handles correlated features.
limitations: Can underestimate importance of correlated features
Results vary with different random seeds
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
import pandas as pd

# Fit model first
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Compute on VALIDATION data
result = permutation_importance(
    model, X_val, y_val,
    n_repeats=30,          # More repeats = more stable estimates
    scoring='roc_auc',
    random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1
)

pi_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'feature': feature_names,
    'importance_mean': result.importances_mean,
    'importance_std': result.importances_std
}).sort_values('importance_mean', ascending=False)

print(pi_df.head(10))
```
